narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your path toward law school, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your bar exam journey as riskier than we can take on—it's not about you as a person, but the patterns it spotted in your profile that echoed too many past stories of struggle. At the top of what stood out was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, placing you at the very bottom of your school's rankings that year, which was the strongest signal pulling toward a higher chance of bar failure, as if the later hurdles in law school hit hard despite earlier efforts. Your LSAT score of 27 followed close behind, signaling to the model a foundational challenge in the kind of analytical firepower the bar demands, much like others who've stumbled there. Even your Year 1 decile of 3.0, while better, still nudged things toward risk when combined with the rest, painting a picture of inconsistent academic climbs. Your middle family income bracket added another layer, as the model links it to tougher access to the extra support that often tips the scales for bar success. Being Asian and female, while strengths in so many ways, aligned in the data with profiles that faced steeper odds in this specific arena. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 and part-time status rounded out the concerns, suggesting divided focus and a baseline that might not build fast enough against the bar's intensity. Together, these wove a story where the model pegged your odds of passing at just 34%—a 66% failure risk that's heartbreakingly common in similar paths we've seen falter. It's not a judgment on your potential or drive; many have rewritten these scripts with targeted changes. Consider bolstering that LSAT or seeking full-time immersion—doors like retakes or prep programs could shift your narrative next time. You've got resilience in aiming for law despite part-time realities; lean into that. We're rooting for your comeback story.
